topic: AI Browser Agents in 2026: Comet vs Browser-Use vs Operator Compared

key_points: Side-by-side breakdown of all major agentic browser tools
Covers architectural differences: screen-control vs. browser-native agents
Notes the Amazon lawsuit against Comet
Discusses the ChatGPT Operator-to-Atlas-to-Agent evolution timeline
Separates consumer AI browsers (Comet, Atlas) from developer automation frameworks (Browser-Use, Skyvern)
Covers pricing and target audience for each tool
Covers four major players: Comet, Atlas, Dia, Edge Copilot
Discusses prompt injection as an unresolved frontier problem
Addresses enterprise security concerns in depth
Consumer-facing comparison with ratings for 8+ tools
Differentiates between AI-enhanced browsers and full agentic browsers
Covers UX, speed, and privacy dimensions
Enterprise workflow focus comparing Atlas vs Comet
Frames Atlas as task-execution-first vs Comet as research-accuracy-first
Notes Atlas integrations with Slack, Salesforce, Notion, and Google Drive

stats.fact: Global AI agents market projected to reach $7.63 billion in 2025-2026, growing to $50.31 billion by 2030 at 45.8% CAGR
Global AI browser market projected to expand to $76.8 billion by 2034 at 32.8% CAGR
85% of organizations have adopted AI agents in at least one workflow as of 2026
27.7% of enterprises already using agentic browsers in production (up from near zero two years prior)
Browser-Use achieves 89.1% success rate on WebVoyager benchmark, highest among major tools
OpenAI Operator/ChatGPT Agent achieves 87% on WebVoyager but only 58.1% on WebArena
Browser-Use has 92,320+ GitHub stars as of May 2026, one of the fastest-growing Python AI tools
32% relative increase in malicious prompt injection detections in AI browsers between November 2025 and February 2026
By 2026, 40% of enterprise applications will include task-specific AI agents (Gartner)
Gartner predicts traditional search engine volume will decline 25% by 2026 as users shift to AI chatbots and agents
Browser-Use BU 2.0 model costs approximately $0.005 per task (~$1 per 200 tasks), the cheapest per-task option
Perplexity Comet dropped its paywall in March 2026 and is now free for all users with full agentic features

angles: Category confusion problem: Comet is a browser, Browser-Use is a framework, Atlas is a browser — comparing them is like comparing a car to an engine. Clarify which tool solves which problem.

Prompt injection crisis: OpenAI CISO publicly stated in December 2025 that AI browsers may always be vulnerable to prompt injection. This is the biggest unresolved debate and a genuine buying-decision factor.
Operator postmortem: OpenAI deprecated Operator after just 7 months (most-hyped product of early 2025). Browser-Use built its entire early narrative as the open-source Operator alternative.

Research vs. Execution philosophical split: Comet's thesis is help you know things (cited answers); Atlas/Operator's thesis is help you do things (SaaS task completion). Genuinely different product philosophies.

Browser-native vs. screen-control architecture: Browser-native agents access the DOM directly; screen-control agents control pixels. Community consensus in 2026: browser-native is clearly superior for web tasks.
Amazon vs. Comet lawsuit: Amazon filed the first-ever lawsuit against an agentic browser in January 2026. Legal exposure is now a real buying criterion for commerce use cases.
Open-source movement vs. proprietary moat: Browser-Use at 92k+ GitHub stars represents a genuine open-source alternative. YC backing signals VC confidence in developer-facing tools.
The free tier disruption: Comet is completely free since March 2026. Browser-Use is open-source MIT. Only OpenAI requires a paid subscription for meaningful access.
Enterprise compliance dimension: Comet has SOC 2/GDPR from day one; Browser-Use offers HIPAA/ZDR/on-prem; OpenAI Frontier targets ServiceNow/Snowflake-scale deployments.
keywords: Perplexity Comet vs Browser-Use vs Operator
AI browser agent no coding required
